content.text: Tool permissions control which tools an agent can call, which resources those tools can touch, and whether any call requires human confirmation before executing. Without an explicit permission model, an agent with billing tools can call them in a session intended only for read-only support — and the only barrier is the model's good behaviour, which is not a security control.

attrs.alt: Tool permission model: three gates — role allowlist, scope isolation, confirmation requirement
attrs.title: Tool Permission Three Gates

content.text: Permissions must be enforced in the handler layer, not relied on in the prompt. A system prompt that says "do not call billing tools" is guidance; an allowlist checked at dispatch time is a control.
Define the set of permitted tool names for each role as a plain Python dict. At dispatch time, check the called tool name against the current session's allowlist before invoking the handler.

The allowlist is checked at dispatch time, after the model has decided to call the tool. Even if the model is given the billing tool schema by accident, the dispatch layer refuses the call when the session role is
Role alone is not enough for multi-tenant systems. A support agent with the
role must only be able to read and write records belonging to their assigned customer — not any customer. Encode the scope in a session context object that handlers receive alongside the tool inputs.
from the dispatch layer — the model never touches it. The
check in the response body is defence-in-depth: even if the backend has a bug, the handler rejects data belonging to a different tenant.

Some operations should not execute automatically even when the role permits them: irreversible deletes, large refunds, mass-notification emails. A confirmation gate pauses execution and requires explicit human approval before the handler runs.

from enum import Enum

class ConfirmationLevel(Enum):
    NONE      = "none"        # execute immediately
    LOW_RISK  = "low_risk"    # execute; log for review
    HIGH_RISK = "high_risk"   # pause; require human approval

# Mark tools with their required confirmation level
CONFIRMATION_REQUIRED: dict[str, ConfirmationLevel] = {
    "create_refund":        ConfirmationLevel.HIGH_RISK,
    "void_invoice":         ConfirmationLevel.HIGH_RISK,
    "delete_customer":      ConfirmationLevel.HIGH_RISK,
    "send_bulk_email":      ConfirmationLevel.HIGH_RISK,
    "apply_credit":         ConfirmationLevel.LOW_RISK,
    "update_ticket_status": ConfirmationLevel.NONE,
}

def check_confirmation(
    tool_name: str,
    inputs: dict[str, Any],
    approved: bool = False,
) -> dict[str, Any] | None:
    """
    Returns a PENDING dict if confirmation is needed but not provided;
    returns None if the call can proceed.
    """
    level = CONFIRMATION_REQUIRED.get(tool_name, ConfirmationLevel.NONE)
    if level == ConfirmationLevel.HIGH_RISK and not approved:
        return {
            "status": "PENDING_CONFIRMATION",
            "tool": tool_name,
            "inputs": inputs,
            "message": (
                f"'{tool_name}' requires human approval. "
                "Please confirm before I proceed."
            ),
        }
    return None   # proceed
dict, the dispatcher returns that as the tool result. The model reads it and relays the confirmation request to the user. The user's next message ("yes, proceed") sets
Combining all three gates — role allowlist, scope check, confirmation gate — into a single dispatcher:

import logging
from datetime import datetime, timezone

_AUDIT = logging.getLogger("agent.audit")

def full_dispatch(
    tool_name: str,
    inputs: dict[str, Any],
    ctx: SessionContext,
    registry,
    approved: bool = False,
) -> dict[str, Any]:
    # Gate 1: role allowlist
    if not is_permitted(tool_name, ctx.role):
        _AUDIT.warning(
            "DENIED role=%s tool=%s user=%s",
            ctx.role, tool_name, ctx.user_id,
        )
        return {
            "error": "PERMISSION_DENIED",
            "message": f"Role '{ctx.role}' cannot call '{tool_name}'",
        }

    # Gate 2: confirmation check (before executing anything)
    pending = check_confirmation(tool_name, inputs, approved)
    if pending:
        return pending

    # Gate 3: execute (scope enforced inside the handler via ctx)
    tool_def = registry.get(tool_name)
    if tool_def is None:
        return {"error": "NOT_FOUND", "message": f"Tool '{tool_name}' not registered"}

    result = tool_def.handler(**inputs, ctx=ctx)

    # Audit: log every completed write
    level = CONFIRMATION_REQUIRED.get(tool_name, ConfirmationLevel.NONE)
    if level != ConfirmationLevel.NONE:
        _AUDIT.info(
            "EXECUTED tool=%s user=%s tenant=%s inputs=%s approved=%s ts=%s",
            tool_name, ctx.user_id, ctx.tenant_id,
            inputs, approved,
            datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        )

    return result
to the dispatcher. When a tool returns
, the loop lets the model relay the request to the user; on the next user turn, it parses the approval and retries with

import json
from anthropic import Anthropic

client = Anthropic()

def agent_loop(
    messages: list[dict],
    ctx: SessionContext,
    registry,
    tools_for_api: list[dict],
) -> None:
    # Track pending confirmations: tool_name -> inputs
    pending: dict[str, dict] = {}

    while True:
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
            max_tokens=4096,
            tools=tools_for_api,
            messages=messages,
        )
        if resp.stop_reason != "tool_use":
            break

        messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content})
        tool_results = []

        for block in resp.content:
            if block.type != "tool_use":
                continue

            # Check if user just approved a pending operation
            approved = block.name in pending and pending.get(block.name) == block.input
            if approved:
                del pending[block.name]

            result = full_dispatch(
                tool_name=block.name,
                inputs=block.input,
                ctx=ctx,
                registry=registry,
                approved=approved,
            )

            if result.get("status") == "PENDING_CONFIRMATION":
                pending[block.name] = block.input

            tool_results.append({
                "type": "tool_result",
                "tool_use_id": block.id,
                "content": json.dumps(result),
            })

        messages.append({"role": "user", "content": tool_results})

These three tests verify the three permission gates independently: role denial, confirmation pause, and approved execution. Run them in CI to catch any regression in the permission model without needing integration tests against a live system.
Role allowlists are explicit — if a tool name is not in the list, the call is denied regardless of what the model was told.
Tenant ID and resource allowlists travel with the session, invisible to the model; handlers enforce them before touching data.
instead of executing; only proceed when the user has explicitly approved.
Log tool name, user, tenant, inputs, and approval status with a timestamp.
Enforce in the handler layer, not the prompt.
Permission checks in the system prompt are guidance; checks in the dispatcher are controls.
